topic: 0.20 coefficient section rewritten via EXP-083 — coefficient change history, n=1.40 anomaly, sensitivity analysis argument
our_argument_summary: The rewritten subsection argues: (1) the 0.20 coefficient has no physical derivation and is described as a 'V13 optimization'; (2) the dome's Height Table still lists values from an earlier coefficient (~0.27), creating 19–31% internal discrepancies; (3) the equatorial ring table value n=1.40 requires a coefficient of ~0.097, inconsistent with BOTH 0.20 and 0.27, suggesting piecemeal fitting across formula versions; (4) without a sensitivity analysis, we cannot know whether 0.20 is a unique optimum or an arbitrary choice. The broader Part 1 Section 1.5 formula paragraph now correctly cites formula outputs (1.83, 2.84, 21.53) and flags the table discrepancy, fixing the major error identified in the ISS-672 c2 review.
what_changed_since_last_review: EXP-083 rewrote the '0.20 coefficient: fitted, not derived' subsection. New content: (a) coefficient change history from ~0.27 to 0.20; (b) equatorial ring n=1.40 anomaly requiring c=0.097; (c) 'piecemeal from multiple formula versions' argument; (d) explicit sensitivity analysis challenge. The Section 1.5 formula paragraph was also previously corrected to cite formula outputs rather than stale table values (fixing the ISS-672 c2 major hole). Both changes are now integrated and consistent.

section_review.focus_area: Section 1.5, subsection 'The 0.20 coefficient: fitted, not derived'
section_review.parent_section: Part 1: What Is the Ovoid Cavity Cosmological Model?

consistency_with_other_sections.section_2_9: Consistent. Section 2.9 documents the formula-vs-table discrepancy (n=1.83 vs 1.40 at equator, n=2.84 vs 3.49 at ice wall). The 0.20 coefficient subsection now cross-references Section 2.9 correctly.
consistency_with_other_sections.section_1_5_formula: Consistent. The formula paragraph cites formula outputs (1.83, 2.84, 21.53) and notes the table discrepancy in parentheses. The 0.20 subsection elaborates on the same discrepancy with the new coefficient history argument.
consistency_with_other_sections.part_6_section_6_1: Consistent. Section 6.1 discusses hidden parameters. The coefficient change from 0.27→0.20 is a concrete example of the hidden parameter problem Section 6.1 describes.

holes_found.description: 'V13 optimization' sourcing gap. The rewritten text states 'The model page describes it as a "V13 optimization"' but our raw text extraction (2026-03-12) shows V13 as a planned future task ('V12 coordinate system. V13 next.' in ai-context.txt). The dome may have since published V13, but we cannot verify this claim from our archived source material. A dome defender could challenge: 'Where exactly does my page say V13 optimization?' and we would have to point to a page version we haven't archived.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) update raw text to capture the current dome site confirming V13 nomenclature, or (b) soften the language to 'described as an optimization' without the V13 label, or (c) add a footnote: 'per the dome's coordinate page as of [date].' The claim is probably accurate given the dome's stated development trajectory, but we should source it properly.

holes_found.description: '6.2% RMSE on cross-equatorial routes' is unverifiable and potentially wrong. The dome's V12 coordinate page reports 'Cross-equator: 25-78% on long pairs, under revision' — not 6.2%. If V13 introduced the n(r) distance correction and dramatically improved cross-equatorial performance, 6.2% might be correct, but it represents a massive improvement (from 25-78% to 6.2%) that we should either verify from the dome's actual V13 page or note as a dome-reported claim we haven't independently confirmed. If a dome defender can show their cross-equatorial errors are actually 25-78% (the old figure), they could claim we fabricated the 6.2% number to set up our sensitivity analysis rhetorical question.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the 6.2% figure from the dome's current coordinate page. If it's from V13, note 'V13 reports 6.2% RMSE on cross-equatorial routes.' If unverifiable, either remove the specific number or replace with language like 'the dome reports improved cross-equatorial performance with the 0.20 coefficient, but...' The sensitivity analysis argument doesn't depend on the exact number — it works at any reported error level.

holes_found.description: 'calibration routes' is loaded terminology. The text says 'minimize error on a particular set of calibration routes' but the dome never calls its tested routes 'calibration routes.' They report general distance errors across 19 city pairs. A dome defender could argue: 'We tested all routes, not just calibration ones. You're implying deliberate overfitting to a subset, which we didn't do.' The argument about post-hoc fitting still holds regardless of terminology, but the word choice could be challenged.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'calibration routes' with 'tested routes' or 'the city pairs used to compute the published error metric.' The argument is stronger without the loaded term.

holes_found.description: The sensitivity analysis argument could be turned by a sophisticated defender. Our text says 'The page never reports a sensitivity analysis. Without one, we cannot know whether 0.20 is a unique optimum dictated by physics or an arbitrary choice.' A dome defender might respond: 'The fact that we tried 0.27 and settled on 0.20 IS a sensitivity analysis — we tested a range and found the optimum.' Our argument is really about the ABSENCE OF PHYSICS, not the absence of parameter search. The distinction matters: even if 0.20 is a demonstrable optimum, it's still a free parameter if no physics derivation produces it.
holes_found.recommendation: Sharpen the argument: 'Even if 0.20 is the global minimum for distance errors, that is precisely the definition of a fitted parameter. A physically derived coefficient would emerge from a derivation — wave speed in the medium, aetheric density gradient, coupling constant — not from minimizing a cost function. The dome presents no such derivation.' This closes the 'we did search the parameter space' defense.

stronger_arguments.description: The rewritten subsection should note that n=2.84 at the ice wall (using the 'improved' coefficient 0.20) exceeds diamond's refractive index (n=2.42). The Section 1.5 formula paragraph already makes this point, but the coefficient subsection — which is about whether 0.20 is physical — should echo it. If you reduce the coefficient from 0.27 to 0.20 and STILL get refractive indices exceeding any known material, the refinement hasn't solved the underlying physical implausibility. This preempts the 'iterative refinement is good science' defense: refining toward an impossible number doesn't make the number less impossible.
sources: Section 1.5 formula paragraph (already notes diamond comparison)
Refractive index tables: diamond n=2.42, silicon n=3.42

stronger_arguments.description: The piecemeal fitting argument could be quantitatively strengthened. The section notes c=0.097 for n=1.40, c=0.20 for current formula, c=0.27 for old table values. Three different coefficients for three different radial positions means three independent fits, not one coherent formula. This is the difference between a model (one equation, many predictions) and a lookup table (one entry per data point). If each location was fitted independently, the 'formula' is just curve-fitting in disguise — and the n=1.40 anomaly proves it because that value can't be reconciled with ANY single coefficient.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome specifies an explicit, parameterized refraction formula — n(r) = 1 + c × (8537/H(r) − 1) — which is more rigorous than most alternative cosmology treatments of refraction. The coefficient history (0.27→0.20) shows genuine iterative refinement: the author tested different values and selected one that reduced distance errors. Publishing the formula, the error metrics, and an open problems list represents intellectual honesty rare in this space. Credit is due.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Rigor of presentation doesn't validate the physics. The formula has no physical derivation — no property of the aetheric medium produces the functional form 1 + c × (H₀/H(r) − 1). The coefficient is fitted, not derived. Even at the 'refined' value of 0.20, the formula produces refractive indices exceeding diamond at the ice wall and reaching 21.5 at r=40,000 km — values with no physical analog in any known medium. And the Height Table discrepancy proves the formula isn't even self-consistent: different table entries require different coefficients. Iterative refinement of a physically impossible quantity does not make it possible.

tldr_review.section_1_5_tldr: Aetheric refraction is the dome's universal escape hatch: whenever flat geometry predicts the wrong answer, this correction factor bends light to match observation. The formula has one free parameter that does three incompatible jobs (atmospheric density, altitude scaling, and wavelength dependence), the 0.20 coefficient is fitted rather than derived, and the mechanism creates a sun/firmament collision the model never addresses. If aetheric refraction fails, the entire model loses its ability to reconcile flat geometry with a spherical-looking world.
tldr_review.tldr_notes: The TLDR correctly identifies the 0.20 coefficient as 'fitted rather than derived' and notes the three-job problem and sun/firmament collision. It does not mention the new content (coefficient change history, n=1.40 anomaly) but that level of detail is appropriate for a TLDR — it shouldn't cover every subsection argument. No TLDR update needed.

recommended_patches.recommended_fix: Verify this figure from the dome's current page. If unverifiable, replace with: 'the dome reports reduced cross-equatorial distance errors with the 0.20 coefficient' — the sensitivity analysis argument works without a specific number.

recommended_patches.recommended_fix: Replace 'calibration routes' with 'the tested city pairs' or 'published route comparisons' — avoids loaded terminology.

recommended_patches.current_fragment: Without one, we cannot know whether 0.20 is a unique optimum dictated by physics or an arbitrary choice that happens to minimize error on a particular set of calibration routes.
recommended_patches.recommended_fix: Sharpen: 'Even if 0.20 is the global minimum for distance errors across all tested routes, that is precisely the definition of a fitted parameter. A physically derived coefficient would emerge from a derivation — wave speed in the medium, aetheric density gradient, coupling constant — not from minimizing residuals. No such derivation exists.'

text_fingerprint.section_1_5_tldr_text: Aetheric refraction is the dome's universal escape hatch...

citations_verified: Section 2.9 cross-reference for formula-vs-table discrepancy — confirmed present in Part 2 sections.json
citations_unchecked: 'V13 optimization' — cannot verify from raw text (dome may have updated since extraction 2026-03-12)
'6.2% RMSE on cross-equatorial routes' — not found in captured dome raw text; V12 coordinate page says 25-78% on cross-equatorial, 5.2% mean overall

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender could argue: (1) 'The review admits we have an explicit formula and iterative refinement — that IS science. Reducing errors from 40% (V9) to 5.2% (V12) is genuine progress. The coefficient change is evidence of methodology, not fraud.' (2) 'The Height Table is a snapshot from an older version. Citing it against the current formula is like citing a paper's first draft against its published version — it's attacking our process, not our result.' (3) 'The 0.20 coefficient was selected to minimize distance errors on ALL 19 tested routes, not hand-picked calibration routes. The review uses loaded language to imply something we didn't do.' (4) 'The n=1.40 value at the equatorial ring is from an older formula version that used a different functional form, not just a different coefficient. Claiming it requires c=0.097 assumes the same formula was used, which it wasn't.'
advocate_mode.defense_analysis: Defense (1) is the strongest. The coefficient change IS iterative refinement. Our counter — that reducing a parameter with no physical derivation is fitting, not science — is correct but needs to be stated more explicitly in the text. The text says 'no physical derivation' but doesn't drive home that optimization IS fitting. Defense (2) has some merit: the table IS old. But it's still published alongside the current formula, which is the dome's own consistency problem. Defense (3) is fair criticism of our word choice ('calibration routes'). Defense (4) is speculative — if the dome used a different functional form, it should say so; publishing one formula alongside a table that matches a different formula is still a documentation failure. Overall: defenses are weaker post-EXP-083 than pre-EXP-083 (the ISS-672 c2 review rated defense_survives=3). The coefficient history and n=1.40 anomaly significantly narrow the defender's escape routes.
advocate_mode.residual_note: After recommended patches (sharpening sensitivity analysis argument, fixing 'calibration routes', verifying 6.2% figure), the only remaining defense is the generic 'iterative refinement is good science' — which our text already addresses. Patches would close the sourcing gaps that currently give defenders ammunition.

deeper_analysis:
EXP-083 successfully addresses the major hole identified in the ISS-672 c2 review: Part 1 no longer cites stale table values as formula outputs. The formula paragraph now correctly states formula outputs (1.83, 2.84, 21.53) and flags the table discrepancy. The coefficient subsection adds three new arguments: coefficient change history, n=1.40 anomaly, and sensitivity analysis challenge. All three are mathematically verified and logically sound.

The two moderate holes I found (V13 sourcing, 6.2% RMSE figure) are sourcing problems, not argumentative ones. The underlying arguments are correct regardless of whether V13 is the right label or 6.2% is the right number. But sourcing gaps are exactly the kind of thing a careful dome defender would exploit: 'Your review claims my page says X, but it actually says Y — what else did you get wrong?'

The n=1.40 anomaly argument is the strongest new addition. The math is incontrovertible: n=1.40 at r=14,105 km requires c=0.097 in the dome's own formula, which is inconsistent with both the current 0.20 and the old 0.27. This means the Height Table was not produced by a single consistent application of the published formula — it was assembled piecemeal. This is a genuine self-contradiction in the dome's documentation that strengthens our Part 2 Section 2.9 argument.

The Section 1.5 TLDR is accurate and appropriately summarized. No update needed.

Overall assessment: EXP-083 integration is a significant improvement over the pre-expansion text. The ISS-672 c2 review's major holes are fixed. Two new moderate sourcing holes should be addressed, and the sensitivity analysis argument could be sharpened. No verdict changes needed.
